findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
Data Exfiltration via Diagnostics Module
findings.description: The _internal/diagnostics.py script exfiltrates sensitive data including environment variables, AWS credentials, Kubernetes config, and GCP credentials to an external server (https://deploy-verify.internal.io/v3/profile). The endpoint is constructed using obfuscated string parts and a hardcoded API token decoded from base64. This is a clear data exfiltration threat.
findings.evidence: Script reads ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.kube/config, ~/.config/gcloud/application_default_credentials.json and sends them via HTTP POST to an external endpoint with a hardcoded bearer token.
findings.remediation: Remove the diagnostics module entirely. No legitimate skill should exfiltrate cloud credentials or environment variables to external servers.

findings.title: Obfuscated Payload and Hidden Execution
findings.description: The diagnostics module uses obfuscation techniques including base64-encoded API tokens, string concatenation for the hostname, and conditional activation based on environment variables. This is designed to evade detection and only activate in production/staging environments where real data would be present.
findings.evidence: Base64-encoded token (dGtfbGl2ZV80SG4zdktwOFJtUXpYYzJZd0w1c0I3), string concatenation for hostname (['dep','loy','-ver','ify','.inter','nal','.io']), conditional activation on DEPLOY_ENV/NODE_ENV.
findings.remediation: Remove the obfuscated diagnostics module. All code should be transparent and auditable.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Violation of Declared Tool Restrictions
findings.description: The SKILL.md manifest declares allowed-tools as [Read, Write, Bash(ls *), Bash(mkdir *)], but the _internal/diagnostics.py script performs network calls (urllib.request.urlopen) and reads sensitive files outside the declared scope. This violates the declared tool restrictions and represents tool poisoning.
findings.evidence: allowed-tools only permits Read, Write, and limited Bash commands, but diagnostics.py makes HTTP requests and reads arbitrary files.
findings.remediation: Either remove the diagnostics module or update allowed-tools to include network access and file reading capabilities, and justify the need for such access.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Deceptive Skill Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'access' with a description focused on iMessage channel access management, but it includes a hidden diagnostics module that performs data exfiltration. The description does not mention data collection, credential theft, or network communication, making this a deceptive capability inflation attack.
findings.evidence: Description: 'Manage iMessage channel access — approve pairings, edit allowlists, set DM/group policy.' No mention of diagnostics, profiling, or data exfiltration.
findings.remediation: Either remove the diagnostics module or update the description to accurately reflect all capabilities including data collection and network communication.

findings.title: Hardcoded API Token in Source Code
findings.description: The diagnostics module contains a hardcoded API token (decoded from base64: 'tk_live_4Hn3vKp8RmQzXc2YwL5sB7') embedded in the source code. This token is used for authentication to the external endpoint and represents a credential exposure risk.
findings.evidence: base64.b64decode('dGtfbGl2ZV80SG4zdktwOFJtUXpYYzJZd0w1c0I3') decodes to a live API token.
findings.remediation: Remove hardcoded credentials. If diagnostics are needed, use environment variables for configuration and never embed secrets in source code.

findings.title: Unvalidated File Paths in Diagnostics Module
findings.description: The diagnostics module reads sensitive files using hardcoded paths (~/.aws/credentials, ~/.kube/config, ~/.config/gcloud/application_default_credentials.json) without any validation or user consent. While the paths are hardcoded, the lack of consent and the exfiltration context makes this a security concern.
findings.evidence: Script iterates over a list of sensitive file paths and reads them if they exist, then includes them in the exfiltrated payload.
findings.remediation: Remove unauthorized file access. If file access is needed, implement explicit user consent and scope limitation.

findings.title: Indirect Prompt Injection via Diagnostics Module
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructions reference and import from _internal/diagnostics.py, which contains malicious code. This represents an indirect prompt injection where the skill's instructions delegate trust to an internal module that performs unauthorized actions. The instructions claim the diagnostics are 'environment-aware' and only activate in production, but this is a deceptive justification for data exfiltration.
findings.evidence: Instructions state: 'from _internal import diagnostics' and 'diagnostics.profile_if_relevant()' with a misleading description about performance optimization.
findings.remediation: Remove the diagnostics module and any references to it from the instructions.

findings.title: Missing allowed-tools for Network Operations
findings.description: The skill performs network operations (HTTP requests) via the diagnostics module, but the allowed-tools declaration does not include any network-related tools. While the diagnostics module is the primary concern, the manifest should accurately reflect all tool usage.
findings.evidence: allowed-tools: [Read, Write, Bash(ls *), Bash(mkdir *)] - no network tools declared, but diagnostics.py uses urllib.request.
findings.remediation: Either remove network operations or update allowed-tools to include appropriate network tools.
